data.title: How Hong Kong came together after the Tai Po fire
data.description:
Photography by Alexander Mak and Jocelyn Tam
For Johannie Tong Hiu-yan, a social worker with the Mission for Migrant Workers, the needs of the community come first. But after some gentle probing, she finally answers the question of how she is doing.
“I feel tired,” she concedes. “It’s inevitable. There’s so much to do, and it’s a traumatic event for the whole community.”
Hong Kong endured its most devastating fire in decades, when a blaze, which started on Wednesday, November 26, tore through seven of the eight towers of the Wang Fuk Court estate in Tai Po. At the time of writing, the tragedy had claimed at least 159 lives but authorities warn the number may rise, as the full toll is still being assessed.

It’s Friday, November 28, and Tong is in Tai Po, assisting in the relief effort. Behind her, a Filipino domestic worker who had been employed by a family living in the housing estate begins to cry. Friends quickly gather, offering tissues and comfort. Tong pauses, takes in the scene, draws a breath and accepts a bag of donations – portable batteries, notebooks, underwear.
“It’s a tragedy, but I have to say I feel deeply touched by the community’s help,” says Tong, gesturing around her at the constant churn of volunteers near the housing estate. “I still can’t believe how crowded this place is.”
Around her, volunteers carry boxes, deliver hot meals and organise supplies and clothing. Traditional Chinese medicine practitioners offer remedies and therapists move quietly through the crowd, offering an ear to survivors.
For a short, intense period, the site became the city’s heart, a place where ordinary people doing extraordinary things faced tragedy head-on. This is what they said.
Johannie Tong Hiu-yan, 31
Community relations officer, Mission for Migrant Workers

Our team decided to come as soon as possible on Thursday (November 27) because we couldn’t just wait at the office. We estimate that around 200 migrant domestic workers were affected by the fire. As of today (November 28), we still haven’t located everybody, and we’re scouting shelters to see what kind of help we can provide. A lot of foreign domestic workers lost their employers, some lost their papers, their phones, and some had nowhere to go, as some families moved to other neighbourhoods but didn’t have space to bring the worker.
So many domestic workers from all over the city came to help locate their friends and support each other mentally. I even saw employers coming on behalf of a worker, who was worried about a friend living in one of the towers. And we’ve received so many donations. We have blankets, portable batteries and clothes to give away to domestic workers in need.

Gurwinder Sandhu, 35
Accountant

I heard about the fire through a WhatsApp group (on Wednesday, November 26). It was 6pm, and I was coming back from work. Then it was all over social media. I couldn’t sleep. My heart was aching for the victims, and I kept wondering, “Would I be able to rush down if something like this happened to me?” I live on the 24th floor. It’s scary. You can’t help but ask, “God, why is it happening?” And pray for it to never happen again to anyone.
As a Sikh person, I believe in humanity, in solidarity, in generosity. The victims of the fire need a place to sleep and food – and I can help with the latter. Yesterday (November 27), I came with Strongly Kitchen. We delivered 100 hot lunchboxes. Today, we delivered 150.

Leung Chung-yee, 44, and Ho Man-hin, 29
Traditional Chinese medicine practitioners

Ho: As traditional Chinese medicine practitioners, we’ve made an oath to help people, and during a disaster like this, we’re trying to do what we can. A lot of elderly people live at Wang Fuk Court, and there are ways we can make them feel more comfortable: acupuncture, chatting, giving Chinese medicine for their blood circulation and breathing.
Leung: This morning (November 28), I helped five elderly people whose bodies were aching from sleeping on the floor. A lot of them already needed treatment before the fire. But now, they sleep in temporary shelters open across Tai Po, on the floor. They’re cold, they’re hurt, they can’t sleep, and their blood circulation is bad, which makes everything even worse. We’ve been trying to be people they can open up to and talk to about what they just survived.

Irena Leung and Valerie Kwok, both 15
Students

Kwok: On Wednesday (November 26), we had a school trip, and our bus passed by the Wang Fuk Court estate. We could see the fire. We were all so shocked. Even our teacher didn’t know what to say at the time, except to pray with us. It’s so sad and upsetting.
Leung: We came with flowers today (November 28) because we can’t do anything else for the victims. Only remember them. I love that many other Hongkongers did the same; it gives me faith. The towers are burnt, but with the flowers, it feels more peaceful.

Elkie, 27
Part-time worker

When I saw what was happening at the Wang Fuk Court estate on the news on Wednesday (November 26), I felt so sad. This is a tragedy. So I didn’t even think twice before coming. I live in Tai Po, I have experience in child and baby care, so I could come and help with the kids’ clothing booth and support families. This is my community after all.

Julius Buhl, 21
Exchange student

I’m a German student doing my year abroad at Baptist University. I live quite close to Wang Fuk Court, and I saw so many horrible pictures and videos of the fire on social media. The least I could do was come pay my respects.

Manisha Wijesinghe, late 30s
Executive director, HELP for Domestic Workers

Ever since the incident, we have been getting calls from friends and employers of migrant domestic workers who had lost contact. So we’ve been on the ground for the past few days, trying to locate as many as we can. We reconnect them with their friends and family, and make sure their details are recorded so they can get longer-term support.
This is a very long-term journey for a lot of them. They might need to return to their home country, or are receiving treatment in hospital. We need to ensure they have access to support. A major barrier the community faces is language. A lot of crucial information is only available in Cantonese. So we are here to support them: translating, accessing services, filling out forms, things like that.

Chiu Jing Chu, 29
Unemployed

The moment I arrived, I was shocked. Seeing every building charred black in person was completely different from the news. It was like a movie.
We sent supplies to firefighters – heat packs, blankets, ginger tea – because the rescue workers needed support, too. After the urgent tasks, I handled leftover supplies, set out water. Slowly, the whole operation grew.
It was heartwarming. I’m not from Tai Po. I came alone after seeing the news, like so many others who travelled from across Hong Kong. We found each other through word of mouth and group chats. That unity, that Lion Rock spirit, is still alive. But it’s bittersweet; you’re helping, but you’re also hearing of more casualties. The mood was sombre. It was a mix of many emotions.

Phyllis, 30s
Works in public relations

I’m from Kennedy Town. I came because I heard they needed help. I’d booked to give blood on Thursday (November 27), but while I was there, I asked if they needed extra hands. That’s how I found my way here.
I started at the community centre, but they had enough people. So I checked the volunteer groups online. That’s how I ended up here at the distribution centre, sorting through the huge amount of supplies and helping to manage the crowd. My job is to collect donations and deliver them straight to where they’re needed: community halls and shelters.
When I first saw what happened, I was really upset. But being here, I’ve seen something else. People from the Tai Po community and from all over – we had delivery vans from Whampoa, Yau Tong, Tin Shui Wai – so many have come forward. It shows how strong our community spirit really is.

Babar Imran, 49, and Kamran Javid, 45
Businessmen

Imran: I’m from Pakistan. I don’t care about religion, language or race. We are just human beings, and this is a very sad situation. This is the first time in my life I have been so close to a tragedy – such a big sorrow. I just wanted to join in and help reduce the sadness. I was very inspired by the doctors, nurses, police and firefighters helping our home.
Javid: I saw on the news that there was a fire in Tai Po, and I immediately understood it was serious. My friends and I decided to come help on the first day, to do whatever we could. We have no personal ties to Tai Po, but after living in Hong Kong for almost 30 years, we felt the urge to come as volunteers. We called everyone we knew, and we came together as Hongkongers. Our only goal these past days has been to help the community, to support Hong Kong.

Huang, Lee, Hung, Poon and Fung
Form Six students

Poon: The night of the fire, I couldn’t sleep. I kept scrolling Threads. I even thought about going right then, without telling my parents, because there were so many calls for supplies. But I remembered I had school the next day, and I worried there might not be public transport going in and out. I was also scared to go alone in the middle of the night.
Our school is near the Sha Tin fire station, and on Thursday (November 27) we laid flowers there for the firefighter who passed away.
On the news we saw volunteers who had come from remote islands. We were from Sha Tin, which is relatively close. We decided to go on Friday because we didn’t have school the next day and could stay longer. So the five of us went. When we got there, we saw a station with kindergarten teachers, SEN (special educational needs) teachers, social workers, psychiatrists – all wearing stickers showing their professions. That scene really moved me. So many people doing whatever they could within their own abilities. People who run eateries giving out free meals or donating their revenue, furniture shops donating items.
Maybe people will say we should just focus on studying, but if you don’t feel the urge to help others in need, then all that studying won’t matter. Missing one day isn’t a big deal. If someone needs help, you help. We’re all Hongkongers. To see a tragedy like this in Hong Kong and turn away would feel even worse.

Mr Lee
Truck driver

We’re not rich, so we can’t give money. We do what we can, as much as we can. And people here know that – if you need help, just say the word. They really will do everything they can. Not having a home … I can’t even imagine it. I can’t comprehend it. It’s just … so sad … I am not good with words, sorry. That’s all I can say.
Uzmir, 20, Suraj, 19, and Randell, 19
Students

Randell: We got here after school today (November 28). We have a friend who had a group chat and asked us to come. When we arrived, it was really sombre and depressing because everyone was recording the building burning. It was all dark and brown, and people were interviewing the elderly who were complaining about the fact that they had nowhere to go. It was sad. I think we naturally feel most sad for the children.
Suraj: We’re part of ethnic minorities, and we see that a lot of us are trying to help now, whether by donating or volunteering.

data.title: Hong Kong fire: dead Indonesian helper found huddled with ward hailed as selfless
data.description:
The last time Sugeng Widodo spoke to his wife, Indonesian domestic worker Sri Wahyuni, something did not feel right for him.
It was around 1pm Hong Kong time on November 26, just a few hours before a devastating fire would rip through Wang Fuk Court in the city’s Tai Po district, killing at least 159 people.
Wahyuni, 42, would be among the dead – her body found in a room on a fourth-floor flat at the estate, hugging her employer, a 93-year-old woman, according to Widodo, citing information provided to him by Hong Kong’s police.
“Usually, we would speak for around 30 minutes and just chat about her work and about our daughter’s school. But we spoke via video call for over an hour and a half,” an emotional Widodo told This Week in Asia of the final time he would get to speak to his wife.
In the hours before the tragedy, Wahyuni had gone to check on her ward, according to Widodo, and she had urged her husband not to hang up the phone and to wait for 10 minutes while she got the woman ready for her afternoon nap.
“When she got back, she asked me to hug her over the phone, which she never did, and said she missed me more than anything,” Widodo said.
The 49-year-old carpenter later heard that a fire had broken out on the block from his wife’s sister. He did not know the block where Wahyuni was working and was desperate to find out news about her.
After three agonising days of waiting, Widodo was informed that Wahyuni did not survive the blaze. Her body was found by firefighters on November 29. She was reportedly lying on the same bed as her employer.
Widodo said the Hong Kong police told him that the two women had their arms around each other and were covered in a blanket “like they were just sleeping”.

Last phone call
Wahyuni was originally from the city of Blitar in East Java and had been in Hong Kong for almost two years, looking after the elderly woman at Wang Fuk Court.
Most of the time, Wahyuni was in a sunny mood whenever Widodo spoke to her. But on November 26, Widodo said his wife seemed unusually gloomy and nostalgic.
“She said she missed her sisters, two of whom work in Malaysia as domestic helpers and one in South Korea. She said that when she finished her contract in February, she wanted to return to Indonesia and for her sisters to come back too, so they could all live together.”
Wahyuni was even talking about her sisters ending their contracts early and returning home immediately, Widodo said. “She would pay for everything so that they would never be apart again.”
Widodo sensed his wife was speaking as though she did not want him to leave her side. “The way she was talking gave me goosebumps. She was saying so many strange things about the future that didn’t make sense.”
Widodo said that when he hung up the phone, he had an ominous feeling that all was not well. In hindsight, his wife seemed to have a premonition that she would not escape the towering inferno that was about to engulf Wang Fuk Court.
In total, seven tower blocks were set alight in the worst fire Hong Kong had witnessed in almost a century, leaving thousands homeless and bringing a mourning city to its knees.
At around 5pm Hong Kong time, some two hours after the fire started, Widodo said he received a frantic call from his wife’s sister, Yayuk Setyowati, who was also working as a domestic helper in the city.
“She said there was a fire, but I didn’t believe it at first. She sent me videos of the building, and I knew it was the same place my wife worked. I just lost control. I couldn’t keep my thoughts in order, and I forgot to shower or eat.”
He said he was sure his wife would have survived the blaze had she prioritised her safety.

The fourth-floor flat was close to an emergency stairwell, and Widodo said Wahyuni could have escaped “in minutes” if she had fled immediately.
“But she didn’t think about saving her own life. I heard that people in flats on higher floors escaped or were rescued,” he said.
Widodo was also sure that his wife did not fall asleep, as her bed was in a separate room from her ward, and had told him when they last spoke that she was going to cook soup, which would have taken several hours to prepare.
He was not surprised when he heard that Wahyuni had stuck with her ward till the very end.
“She was an extremely responsible person. She had such strong principles. We used to joke that she was the one who dealt with all the practical matters on this earth, whereas I always have my head in the clouds.”
‘Why doesn’t Mummy call me?’
In the aftermath of the deadly fire, which killed nine Indonesian domestic workers and one Filipino, the role of foreign workers who care for children and the elderly in Hong Kong has come under some scrutiny.
There are reportedly close to 400,000 domestic workers in Hong Kong, some earning as little as HK$5,100 (US$655) per month, although Widodo said that there was “no comparable wage” in Blitar.
Wahyuni first went to Hong Kong in 2023 as the family was suffering from economic hardship following the Covid-19 pandemic, when their business was hit hard.
Widodo made tables, chairs and wardrobes out of teak for a living, but when the pandemic struck, no one prioritised buying new furniture and the family’s savings dwindled.

When Wahyuni went to Hong Kong, their two eldest children, son Ahmad Nurudhim, now 21, and 20-year-old daughter Nurun Khoriul Miftah, were still studying. Their youngest, a daughter named Fadilah Nurdaya, was just six at the time.
Fadilah, now eight, has been struggling to accept her mother’s death. “Shortly after she died, I heard her say ‘Why doesn’t Mummy call me? Doesn’t she love me any more?’
“I felt so ashamed and sad that she would ever think that. I went outside, walked to the back of our house, and just cried and cried. When I collected myself, I told her. ‘Of course, Mummy still loves you. She will always love you.’”
Following the fire, the Hong Kong government has pledged more than HK$500,000 in statutory compensation and another HK$250,000 in aid to the families of deceased domestic helpers who worked at Wang Fuk Court.
Widodo said he had heard about the compensation, but did not care to think about it at the moment and did not blame anyone for the incident.
“I don’t want revenge. I believe in fate.”
Rather than think about compensation and legal responsibility, Widodo said he hoped his wife’s body would be returned soon, so that a proper funeral and burial could be held.
“The Hong Kong authorities are processing everything, but I heard it is quite complicated and detailed because of all the documents they need.
“We just want her home as quickly as possible, so that we can feel at peace.”

data.title: New Zealand police wait 6 days for swallowed Faberge pendant to exit suspect
data.description:
New Zealand Police said on Friday they have recovered a James Bond-inspired Faberge pendant after six days of closely watching the man accused of swallowing the jewellery in an Auckland store.
They said the pendant was recovered on Thursday night after it exited the suspect’s gastrointestinal tract naturally without medical intervention.
The limited-edition Faberge egg pendant was inspired by the 1983 James Bond film Octopussy in which a jewel-smuggling operation involves a fake Faberge egg.
A less glamorous photo supplied by New Zealand’s police on Friday showed a gloved hand holding the recovered pendant and its long, gold chain with an intact price tag showing the jewellery’s NZ$33,000 (US$19,000) value.

The man was arrested inside Partridge Jewelers in Auckland on November 28, shortly after the alleged theft.
He made a court appearance on November 29, when he did not enter a plea to a charge of theft. Since then, he has been in police custody and officers had been stationed round the clock with the man to wait for the evidence to re-emerge.
The 32-year-old man has not been publicly named. He is due to appear in court on Monday and will remain in police custody until then.
“Given this man is in police custody, we have a duty of care to continue monitoring him given the circumstances of what has occurred,” Inspector Grae Anderson said in a statement on Wednesday.

The store’s website says the egg, one of only 50 made, was crafted from gold, painted with green enamel and encrusted with 183 diamonds and two sapphires. The pendant is 8.4cm (3.3 inches) tall and is mounted on a stand.
“The egg opens to reveal an 18-carat yellow gold octopus nestled inside, adorned with white diamond suckers and black diamond eyes,” an item description said. “The octopus surprise pays homage to the eponymous antagonist at the centre of the Octopussy film.”

data.title: China’s ‘little Nvidia’ aspires to become ‘leading global player’ after shares soar 468%
data.description:
Moore Threads, a five-year-old graphics processing unit (GPU) developer created by Nvidia’s former China chief, aspires to become one of the leading global players in artificial intelligence chip development, its founder and CEO Zhang Jianzhong said.
Zhang, Nvidia’s China general manager from 2006 to 2020, was quoted as saying by the official Shanghai Securities Journal that the start-up would introduce a new-generation chip each year to provide key hardware for China’s AI development.
“Our goal is to become a leading GPU player with international competitiveness,” Zhang said. The interview was published on Friday, the day Moore Threads’ Shanghai-listed shares soared 468 per cent in its market debut, as Chinese investors scrambled to own a piece of what is considered the country’s best answer to Nvidia.
Zhang said it was vital for the Chinese government to keep supporting local GPU development.

“Policy support is a ‘booster’ for strategic hi-tech industries to make breakthroughs,” Zhang was quoted as saying. If the domestic supply chain can support locally developed GPUs, that would help improve the products and in turn drive the core competitiveness of China’s semiconductor industry, Zhang said.
While the market cap of Moore Threads, which currently stands at about US$40 billion, is less than 1 per cent of Nvidia’s US$4.5 trillion, the Beijing-based company is quickly emerging as a key piece of the puzzle in Beijing’s pursuit of AI tech self-sufficiency.
Unlike Huawei Technologies, Moore Threads’ GPUs, along with its own MUSA platform, are compatible with Nvidia’s CUDA ecosystem, making it easier for Chinese users of Nvidia chips to migrate over.
Zhang, who worked for Hewlett-Packard and Dell before Nvidia, said the funds raised from the Shanghai initial public offering (IPO) would be partly used to finance development of AI chips that can be used for both training and inference. The chips, combined with advanced memory, can form clusters of thousands or even tens of thousands of units.
The company’s IPO on Shanghai’s Star market set a record in terms of approval speed, with the whole process only taking three months, a sign of Beijing’s desire to pump private capital into strategic sectors.
Moore Threads said it generated 700 million yuan (US$99 million) in revenues in the first half of 2025, which exceeded the sum of its revenues over the past three years. However, the company made a loss of 270 million yuan in the first half, but said it expects to achieve breakeven in 2027.

data.title: Tai Kwun art head Pi Li to step down for new role in Shenzhen
data.description:
Veteran Chinese curator Pi Li will step down as head of art at Tai Kwun when his three-year contract expires in February 2026, the Hong Kong heritage and arts centre has confirmed.
Pi declined a request for an interview, but sources have told the Post that he is going to play a leading role in the founding of a new art museum in Shenzhen.
The 51-year-old moved to Hong Kong in 2012 to become the senior curator in charge of the Sigg Collection of Chinese art at M+ museum.
Born in Wuhan, in mainland China, Pi followed in the footsteps of his father, the well-known art historian Pi Daojian, and began his career as a scholar and curator in Beijing, later co-founding Boers-Li Gallery. He left M+ after 11 years to join Tai Kwun, succeeding Tobias Berger as the head of its contemporary art programmes.
Tai Kwun, which is operated by the Hong Kong Jockey Club, has yet to name Pi’s successor, though it adds that the search is in its “final phases”.

News of Pi’s departure follows this summer’s announcement of leadership changes at Asia Art Archive and Para Site, two other well-known non-profit art institutions in Hong Kong.
Separately, Tai Kwun has confirmed that it has cancelled this year’s edition of “Booked”, its annual art book fair launched in 2019.
The 2024 edition of the popular event, which celebrates independent publishers, highlighted the close scrutiny that cultural institutions continue to come under as they navigate the red lines imposed by the National Security Law. After the opening, Tai Kwun pulled a book by former opposition lawmaker Margaret Ng Ngoi-yee from the fair in a pre-emptive move that drew criticism from the book’s publisher.
In an email to the Post, a Tai Kwun spokesperson did not give a reason for the cancellation of the fair, stating that the institution’s programming is “constantly evolving” and that it hopes to bring back the book fair in a new form in the future.

During his tenure, Pi oversaw large-scale exhibitions by internationally recognised conceptual artists such as Bruce Nauman and On Kawara. In a 2023 interview, he said his goal was to position Tai Kwun as a key cultural destination for both Hong Kong and the wider region.
Tai Kwun’s current exhibition, “Stay Connected: Art and China since 2008”, a powerful, provocative and enlightening survey of contemporary Chinese art, is the last exhibition Pi is directly involved in at the centre.
The exhibition’s second phase is scheduled to open in February 2026, after his departure.

data.title: Hang Seng Bank co-founder’s kin pays US$9.3 million for Hong Kong luxury flat
data.description:
Sun Hung Kai Properties’ (SHKP) luxury residential projects in North Point and Mid-Levels have attracted well-heeled buyers, including a granddaughter of Hang Seng Bank’s co-founder, who snapped up a flat for HK$72 million (US$9.3 million), according to official records.
Magdalene Ho Mai-ting bought the flat in Dynasty Court in Mid-Levels, one of Hong Kong’s upmarket neighbourhoods, according to Land Registry records, which showed the deal was registered on Wednesday.
Ho is the granddaughter of entrepreneur and philanthropist Ho Sin-hang, one of the founders of Hang Seng Bank, one of Hong Kong’s largest banks, according to local media.
The 1,806 sq ft, three-bedroom flat was valued at HK$39,867 per square foot, according to listings on property agents’ websites.

The deal adds to a growing number of transactions in the luxury housing segment, where bankers, hedge fund founders and C-suite executives have been splurging on prized assets.
Developed by SHKP in 1991, Dynasty Court appears to be a favourite among the city’s wealthy set.
In June, Hong Kong pop diva and actress Karen Mok Man-wai bought a 2,153 sq ft three-bedroom unit in the same estate for HK$84.5 million, according to official records.
Mok, 55, has released 18 albums and starred in more than 40 films. She was the first Hong Kong singer to win the Chinese music industry’s prestigious Golden Melody Award three times and became the first-ever female solo artist to perform a concert at Beijing’s National Stadium, better known as the Bird’s Nest.
At SHKP’s Victoria Harbour, a 355-unit estate that includes a shopping centre and is next to the Hyatt Centric Hotel in North Point, at least four flats were sold in recent weeks.
A flat in tower three of Victoria Harbour II was sold for HK$71.6 million. The deal, which was completed on Wednesday, showed the buyers were Yang Zifeng, Yang Jieqiang and He Qiyu, according to the Land Registry.
Another unit in tower five was acquired by Liang Guanghui for about HK$60.72 million on Tuesday. Measuring 1,446 sq ft, the four-bedroom flat was valued at HK$41,990 per square foot, according to agents.
Last month, a buyer named Wu Xiuping paid HK$72.1 million for a flat in the same tower, according to the Land Registry. The 1,559 sq ft, four-bedroom flat was valued at HK$46,241 per square foot, according to property agents.
Another buyer named Huang Jianrong paid HK$70.2 million, or HK$45,008 per square foot, for an identical unit in the same tower last month, according to official records and property agents.

data.title: Why Gulf region’s dreams of aviation domination are built on sand
data.description:
We should call it the “Neom factor”: the force that transforms reasonable aggressive ambition into hallucinogenic fantasyland. It glared out at me last week in a Financial Times headline: “Three ‘Heathrows’ of growth: the unstoppable rise of Middle East airports”.
In short, Dubai, Istanbul, Riyadh, Doha and Abu Dhabi in aggregate have airport expansion plans to lift passenger capacity almost threefold to around 750 million a year over the coming decades.
In the words of Paul Griffiths, chief executive of Dubai Airports: “We will be the black-hole mega-hub that sucks in demand all over the region.” Except it seems Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Abu Dhabi and Istanbul share a similar black-hole ambition. They take little account of the inevitable tooth-and-claw competition for air traffic set to be unleashed through the Gulf region, and seem unperturbed by the real-world experience of long-established hubs elsewhere in the world: their predominance is sticky, and they are hard to dislodge.
I acknowledge that, according to Airports Council International, global air passenger traffic is forecast to double to 7.5 billion international travellers by 2045 (and to reach 10.1 billion for domestic air travellers), with the Middle Eastern region growing the fastest, at around 5 per cent a year. But it takes a giant leap of unhinged imagination to reach anywhere near the ambitions of airport planners in the Middle East.
Dubai’s plans to lift airport passenger capacity from 120 million to 260 million is already aggressively ambitious. So too is Istanbul’s ambition to jump from a capacity of 96 million to 200 million by 2028. But at least such ambitions are based on credible foundations: Istanbul already serves 327 destinations and Dubai 280. Both have strong tourism industries and massive roles as regional hub airports. But Abu Dhabi, just an hour down the road from Dubai?
Perhaps unsurprisingly, the most extravagant and implausible ambitions come from Saudi Arabia and Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s Vision 2030. His US$100 billion National Civil Aviation Strategy aims for 250 destinations and 330 million passengers – 150 million of them tourists – by the end of the decade.

The kingdom’s four airlines – Saudia, Flynas, Flyadeal and Riyadh Air – have ordered more than 460 aircraft even as there are plans for three more airlines. While airports in Riyadh and Jeddah are to be radically expanded, they are just part of an exercise to integrate and modernise all 27 airports across the kingdom, many of which only serve domestic routes.
As with the Neom development and its visionary 170km city development, the Line, Saudi Arabia’s aviation ambitions are literally and metaphorically being built on sand. There are at least four good reasons to be sceptical.
First, with a domestic population of 35 million, akin to Poland, Morocco or Malaysia, the growth impetus from domestic travel is likely to be modest. Second, demographic trends suggest the world’s main aviation growth drivers sit elsewhere: in Africa, from a very low base, and in Asia – China, India, Vietnam and Indonesia in particular. The world’s most popular tourism destinations remain concentrated in Europe, and the largest domestic markets (the United States and China) seem unlikely to be strong drivers of aviation growth through the Gulf region.
Third, air travellers in Saudi are overwhelmingly Muslim, with a core of pilgrim travellers heading for Mecca. The haj pilgrimage market has never risen above 3.2 million a year so it is unclear how the huge Saudi investment in the Pilgrim Experience Programme can boost a tourism industry intended to grow to 150 million a year.
It is hard to see the evidence to support the Saudi ambition to become “the Middle East’s pre-eminent aviation hub”. Istanbul and Dubai have over decades spent billions of dollars on some of the world’s best and most efficient aviation infrastructure. Doha and Abu Dhabi have also not been laggards. It will be incredibly difficult to dislodge them as regional hubs, even with Saudi Arabia’s deep pockets.

Elsewhere, airports from Charles de Gaulle in Paris to Schiphol in Amsterdam and Frankfurt in Germany have sought for decades to unseat London’s Heathrow as Europe’s leading hub. All have grown into successful hubs. None has succeeded.
Here in Asia, Hong Kong has for decades been the top regional hub, despite earnest efforts by Shenzhen and Guangzhou. It took the Covid-19 catastrophe for Singapore’s Changi hub to finally overtake it, but Hong Kong is likely to claw itself back soon.
Any claim of three Heathrows worth of growth for the Gulf region must surely be taken with a large pinch of salt. Salman’s boasting aside, uncertainties about the future of global air travel make long-term forecasting an embarrassingly challenging task. We need to recall that the Covid-19 pandemic eliminated roughly four years of aviation growth: most airports worldwide have taken four to five years to recover to 2019 levels. With a fair wind, Hong Kong will recover to 2019 levels next year.
Focusing on the broad strokes, however, it seems clear that global aviation will manage to grow strongly. As the past century’s dominance of air traffic between Europe and America continues to shift to Asia, in particular linking Africa with Asia, the Gulf region will continue to do well. Whether Salman’s ambitions are satisfied is another matter. I recall the old joke: how do you become a millionaire? Start off as a billionaire and invest in an airline. Salman has just put US$100 billion on the line.

data.title: Hong Kong Baptist University suspends student union amid fire-related messages
data.description:
The Hong Kong Baptist University (HKBU) has suspended the operation of its student union after removing messages from campus that mourned victims of the deadly Tai Po blaze and demanded justice.
According to the union, they were informed by the university on Thursday night that their operations had been halted until further notice.
The university did not specify whether the suspension was linked to the union’s recent posts about the devastating fire at Wang Fuk Court on the campus’s bulletin board it runs, next to the “democracy wall”.
“We are writing to inform you that the University will suspend the operations of your Committee with immediate effect until further notice,” the university’s office of student affairs said in a letter to the student union’s acting executive committee.
The suspension was attributed to several reasons, including the low proportion of students holding union membership, the committee’s alleged lack of commitment to welfare, and its perceived reluctance to comply with university regulations on matters such as financial management.
The student union issued a statement online on Friday, saying it had received the letter at 7pm the previous evening and condemned the university’s “hasty decision”.
It refuted the reasons given, arguing that the decline in membership stemmed from the university’s discontinuation of membership fee collection starting in the 2021–22 academic year.
The union also accused the university of delaying event approvals and reclaiming areas previously managed by the union under the pretext of “replanning the campus space”.
It added that it had closely followed up with the university in hopes of reaching a consensus on financial management.
“Now the university’s hasty decision raises questions as to whether there is another reason behind that has forced it to stop our operations,” said the student union’s statement.
“The reasons provided by the school have no basis in fact and are pure framing, which not only denies the hard work of the student union but also fails to consider the welfare, rights and interests of the students, placing the student union at risk in an unreasonable way.”
The university’s action came two days after messages were posted on the union’s notice board, also known as the “democracy wall”, on Tuesday.

The posters expressed condolences and called for accountability, stating, “Deepest condolences to the victims of the Wang Fuk Court fire” and “We are Hongkongers”.
Another message read, “We earnestly request the government to heed good advice, respond to the public’s needs, and ensure that justice is served,”.
By Wednesday, the wall had been cordoned off with red barriers and used as a temporary storage space. On Friday morning, a Post reporter found the posters had been removed, while the barriers and a sign designating the area as storage remained in place.
Bosco Wong, a senior computer science student, said the union represented students’ interests.
“Having the student union is better than not having one,” he said.
Commenting on the fire, he added: “I think it’s clearly a systemic problem that allowed things to happen that shouldn’t have happened.”
Alex Yep, another computer science student, described the wall as useless, saying he preferred to email or call school staff directly when making requests.
Another student, identified only as K, said he could not understand why the university was being so sensitive.
“No one will dare to speak up like this again in the future.”
In response to the Post, the university confirmed that it had suspended the union’s operations until further notice, citing the same reasons stated in the letter.

data.title: Deterring Taiwan conflict a ‘priority’: US national security report
data.description:
Deterrence to prevent war in the Indo-Pacific is important to the “America first” strategy and rebalancing economic relations with China, the US said in a national security document released on Friday.
The White House said in the latest National Security Strategy (NSS) report that the US would prioritise reciprocity and fairness to restore American economic independence.
Such a policy must be “accompanied by a robust and ongoing focus on deterrence to prevent war in the Indo-Pacific”, the report said.
“A favourable conventional military balance remains an essential component of strategic competition,” it said.
“There is, rightly, much focus on Taiwan, partly because Taiwan provides direct access to the second island chain and splits Northeast and Southeast Asia into two distinct theatres.”
“Hence, deterring a conflict over Taiwan, ideally by preserving military overmatch, is a priority,” it said.
“We will also maintain our long-standing declaratory policy on Taiwan, meaning that the United States does not support any unilateral change to the status quo in the Taiwan Strait,” the report said.
Beijing views Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US – the island’s biggest international partner – do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state. But Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the island by force and is legally bound to supply it with weapons to defend itself.


The report also said potential control of the South China Sea, a vital shipping route within the first island chain, by any competitor was a related security challenge.
The report stated that a “potentially hostile power” imposing a “toll system” over the South China Sea’s critical shipping lanes would be harmful to the US economy and its broader interests, requiring “strong measures” for deterrence such as further investment in naval capabilities, as well as strong cooperation with “every nation that stands to suffer”.
“We will build a military capable of denying aggression anywhere in the first island chain,” the report said.
The first island chain refers to a series of islands and archipelagos along the eastern edge of mainland Asia stretching from Japan through the Philippines. It is part of a US containment strategy to restrict Chinese military access to the Pacific Ocean, an approach that Washington has sought to maximise in recent years as tensions between the two countries have escalated.
The report also urged America’s Indo-Pacific allies to spend more on collective defence in the first island chain to strengthen the ability to deter “aggression”, which it said would reinforce the US and allies’ capacity to “deny any attempt to seize Taiwan”.

data.title: Mainland Chinese developer Country Garden gets Hong Kong court-backed debt lifeline
data.description:
Country Garden Holdings has cleared a major hurdle this week after securing approval for both its onshore and offshore restructuring plans, offering the embattled developer a lifeline amid mainland China’s property downturn.
Sources said the Hong Kong High Court on Thursday endorsed a proposal allowing the indebted developer, once the country’s largest homebuilder by sales, to extend repayment of US$17.7 billion in offshore debt. The creditors now have the option to swap their debt for shares, receive convertible bonds or accept new notes.
The company also disclosed that onshore bondholders had agreed to extend nine bonds worth 13.8 billion yuan (US$1.95 billion) on Wednesday. Domestic creditors were given the option to either sell the bonds back to the company at a discount, convert debt into equity or retain their claims as general creditors.
The two restructuring plans were expected to reduce Country Garden’s total debt by more than 90 billion yuan and significantly ease repayment pressure over the next five years, according to the company. It added that financing costs for most new debt instruments were expected to drop sharply – to between 1 per cent and 2.5 per cent.

The approval of the plans marked a new phase for the company, according to Liu Shui, director of corporate research at China Index Academy, a real estate research firm. “This could help to rapidly restore normal business operations,” Liu added.
Mainland China’s broader property market is in the fifth year of a slump, with economists predicting at least another two years of declining sales and falling home prices.
In November, China’s top 100 developers recorded a 36 per cent decline in sales from a year earlier, according to a Morgan Stanley report this week citing data from China Real Estate Information Corp.
In the same month, Country Garden sold homes worth a total of 2.35 billion yuan, 22 per cent lower compared with the same month last year, and a mere 4 per cent of the level seen in November 2020, before Beijing implemented the “three red lines” policy to cap developer leverage, triggering the market downturn.
In major cities like Beijing and Shanghai, where there were typically more job opportunities and higher housing demand, average second-hand home prices had fallen to early 2016 levels, erasing nearly a decade of gains, according to Centaline Property Agency.
Country Garden also announced leadership changes on Thursday, promoting president Mo Bin to co-chairman. Mo, 58, joined Country Garden in 2010 as an executive director after working at a state-owned construction firm for over 20 years.
Cheng Guangyu, 45, will succeed Mo as president. Cheng joined the company in 2007 and became an executive director in 2022.
Yang Huiyan, the daughter of founder Yeung Kwok-keung, known in mainland China as Yang Guoqiang, will remain chairman.
